sentences.text: How close to Toronto are you?
I said, well, a place called Oakville.
She said, that's funny.
She said I just came from there.
Then we went to the next Lunenberg, and I came in the shop there.
And the guy asked the same kind of questions.
Where you from?
Toronto.
Close to Toronto.
How close?
I said, well, Oakville.
He moved from here to--
I was born January 15, 1928 in Amsterdam.
My father's name was Saloman Mossel, M-O-S-S-E-L.
My mother's name was Jansje, typical Dutch, Mossel van Bingen, V-A-N-B-I-N-G-E-N.
For the first five years I lived in Amsterdam until 1933.
My father was a diamond cutter, and as such, employed.
Not too much-- I was at kindergarten, the Jewish kindergarten in Amsterdam.
I don't know too many memories.
It was an eventful life and your usual childhood occurrences.
When I was five years old, it was then in 1933, as the Depression was on, there was not much business in the diamonds-- factory, the diamond where my father worked.
So he moved to a place of Zandvoort, near-- it's still a seaside resort.
Z-A-N-D-V-O-O-R-T-- where I spent the next five years of my childhood, from 1933 till 1938.
What I can recollect is that this was the happiest time of my childhood.
We lived there.
I was at school, naturally a public school.
I had Hebrew lessons.
I was-- it was a happy childhood.
We were not well off, but we were OK, and there was no problems in that respect.
We made our trips to Amsterdam to visit my grandparents, which were the parents of my father.
My mother's parents were died before I was born.
And so as-- that's the usual childhood.
I was a fan at that time of Shirley Temple.
I looked at every movie from her.
As I said, it was a marvelous time, and the best time of my life.
In 1938, we moved back to Amsterdam in a new complex of houses, which were very modern for that time.
It was originally kind of a cooperative.
There was a very, very nice what you call here a flat.
We were lived on the first floor.
My father still-- we moved back to Amsterdam with my father still, apparently, as far as I can recall.
I was 10 years old.
He didn't have too much work.
He was at that time then unemployed.
And we were on unemployment insurance, which much to the dismay of my mother and father, naturally, who were not used to that and hated every moment of it.
I was at school, in public school.
I went to the public school.
The public school I was-- I had to go there, was situated in a very, very, very well-to-do area.
And I was still a very provincial girl after five years in Zandvoort.
Naive and trusting, I happened to be in a class where a teacher was, who for some reason or other, didn't like me.
I could, as is hindsight now, not sure, but he didn't like me.
Everything he tried to degrade-- not degrade me, to humiliate me in front of the class.
But still, I am sorry to say it has left a lasting impression.
And even sometimes to this day, I feel sometimes those effects.
It was not good for me.
I had several accidents in that school.
I broke my-- fractured my arm.
He had to-- his favorite kids had to walk me to my house, which was quite a distance, which shouldn't have been done.
Another incident was that a boy was stepping on my finger and broke my nail, totally crushed it.
He just ignored it, and I had to wait till it was recess or when we went to home-- went home to let it look after.
Otherwise, I learned and I completed that public school.
And I didn't mind that much, except from him, but it was normal.
Then in 1940, I was 12 years old.
I graduated from public school.
And I entered the Jewish-- and I entered the public high school, where I did fairly well.
And that was already in 1940.
During the time in May 1940, there was the invasion of the Germans in Holland.
Naturally, we knew about that, because we read the papers.
And in September '39, we followed that, and Germany invaded Poland.
And then they came closer and closer.
Then one night, just the night before, my parents were discussing maybe we should move to America.
It was just the night before on the 9th of May, during the night we woke up with a terrific noise, and we heard all of the airplanes coming over.
And all the people we were-- our bedroom was close to around them.
We were standing outside at 3 o'clock during the night.
And we are looking at the sky, and the sky was black from all the German planes invading Holland.
So there's lots of excitement.
And at that time nobody yet felt anything.
The Germans were pretty clever to wait with all actions what they were planning to do.
Life afterwards, I went to school.
And life resembled-- remained or returned to, not say normal, but to almost normal.
And then slowly, but surely, I don't know exactly the events when or what, but then came that we were not allowed to slowly to visit public transit, swimming pools, go to movie theaters.
We were restricted to buy our groceries in stores.
We were issued with a yellow David Star.
The Dutch population reacted very favorable.
In that time, they were spontaneously at that time.
And we had to wear our stars, the whole population of the Dutch population wore the star at solidarity with us.
Naturally, it was short-lived.
But it was a good sign that they were commiserating with us.
They couldn't do that too long.
So then there was also that all the public schools, high school and public schools were closed for the Jews.
We had to go to a separate Jewish high school.
I lived-- Leny, as the Germans were forcing German Jews out of Germany--
Right, in 1938, before the Kristallnacht, we had lots of German Jewish families coming into Holland to fleeing.
And we had one-- my parents took one couple and their little son in our house for a couple of days, a short while, just to help them out.
That's all I know.
They went on their way.
Where and when, I do not recall, but I know they were with us for a short while to help out.
Then I had to-- then we were had to go to the Jewish high school, which was quite a distance from our home.
I had to walk all the way in the morning quite a distance.
I don't know how many miles or kilometers.
I don't know.
But I was at the school.
And that was also, despite the circumstances that I was forced into the school, I felt very, very good being in that Jewish school with all the people.
Because also you could speak freely about what happened.
And in the public high school before, you were always restricted, couldn't say what you wanted about the circumstances, what happened around you.
No, it was-- there were high schools called [NON-ENGLISH] one was the low world height.
It was the little different one what you learned different subjects and was a little bit higher in education.
And so all the Jewish children who were in high school were in the same kind of high school, but only together with the-- with Jews.
They couldn't be mixed together with the non-Jewish kids.
Well, naturally religion, and for the rest, everything else that was in the public school.
We had very competent teachers.
One teacher of religion was-- his name was Yaacov Soetendorp, S-O-E-T-E-N-D-O-R-P.
His son is presently a rabbi in the shul in synagogue in Amsterdam.
He was our religious teacher.
And that's what I remember.
Freer to talk, right.
Freer to talk about how you thought about the Germans and what happened, and that people unfortunately were taken away, were rounded up out of their houses.
That was the sad part.
But it was freer.
That's what I found.
And much more relaxed.
You were between yourselves with Jews.
I don't think that we talked too much about it, because it was too frightful.
Because you see every time and every morning, then you came back to school.
There were also one or more students who didn't come back to school.
And then we knew that they were being deported and taken out of their houses the night before.
So every night when we left we say, maybe, hopefully we see you tomorrow.
And that was naturally depressing, because it became less and less students in the class.
The social life, despite everything else, I had a boyfriend and a girlfriend.
And she had a boyfriend.
And there was a theater in Amsterdam.
Shall I mention a name?
[DUTCH] that means theater, or yes.
And there were-- at that time they were playing musicals like the [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know at the moment.
And all kind of German musicals from court-- not from court, while with all those Jews still playing now.
And they were performing, all those German musicians, and because they were fled Germany, too.
And we were attending in the theater, and we had a good time.
Unfortunately that theater later on was used for a holding-- a holding room for people to be deported to the other camps.
We didn't know that then.
But at that time, we had a good time.
And then one day, my boyfriend came and told me I have to go.
I got my notice.
I have to report for work in Germany under disguise they said.
So we said goodbye.
And you thought, well, you have to work.
He's young.
He'll be returning.
On yeah, this disguise is what I said.
You have to go to Germany to work.
Well, all the time they knew it wasn't camp, concentration camps they were deported to.
But that was the impression we had received in Holland, that people had to work.
Like lots of people, lots of few engaged couples, they married in a hurry so that the wife with the husband, or husband stay and remain with the wife.
If we have to work in eastern Europe, we might as well stay together and be married.
You see, that's why they got a lot of people.
And that's what we thought, too, that he went.
So he left, and his boyfriend left too.
Then another portion about the school, my father-- my grandmother, my father's mother, at that time was ill.
And we had borrowed some supplies from, what is that, a Green Cross, or whatever your supplies you could borrow.
At that time, we didn't need it anymore, because I think she was had to be admitted to the hospital.
So my father and I, we had to walk on a Sunday to the Jewish quarter there, and we returned the supplies.
And in Amsterdam is known for the drawbridges.
And when just before we came to the drawbridge, we were warned, somebody warned us there is a round-up.
The Germans are rounding up people, and better hurry and run.
So my father pushed me over the bridge.
We were on the other side of the bridge.
The bridge was drawn up.
And now the people on the other side were caught like rats, rounded up, and deported to the camps.
That was a favorite pastime of the Germans, to round up the people.
There was a Jewish quarter-- a Jewish, it was a ghetto in Amsterdam.
But the ghetto was not, I think, like it is in Warsaw.
You could freely go in and out.
But it was just designed that for the people there in that neighborhood.
There was nobody else.
All non-Jewish people had to leave there.
That's as far as I know.
Remained as is, as yet the same, but only there were no-- that in case the Germans wanted to have a round-up, they had every rat in the fall.
We were caught.
And they mostly did it on a Friday night, naturally.
We were all registered right.
There was an-- two men, Asher and Cohen, who were-- they led the community.
They didn't lead the community, but the Germans appointed them or they asked for the job.
I don't-- that's as far as I know.
And they had to-- we had all to register.
We had to register in order to get our star.
And every people-- and every Jewish people, dutifully like cattle to slaughter, we were very law-abiding.
We went and we registered.
So they knew where everybody was.
They got a kind of an ID card with a big J on it.
I didn't get that then.
I only received it when I was 15 years old.
So but everybody, an adult 15 and older, had a J so they knew exactly where the Jews were.
And if the Germans were not totally sure, then some non-Jewish people were all too glad to point out where the Jews were.
No, it was just the one spontaneous reaction when the first time the star was introduced and we had to wear them.
Afterwards, they didn't do that anymore.
I'm sure that they were issued punishment if they would do, because it would confuse them.
No, they didn't, not anymore.
Where was I?
Yes, despite everything else, I became in contact with a group of Jewish teenagers like myself.
And we started to frequent the shul, and to have services, and have some social get-togethers, which I liked very much.
That was in the years '41, '42.
In 1941, my grandmother died on February 1941.
It was on that day that there was a huge round-up from all Jewish men in the Jewish ghetto.
The reason was-- the reason was that there was one Dutch fascist was-- they were provoking.
The Dutch fascist was provoking, coming into the Jewish quarters.
And he was provoke, and he was killed by the Jewish guys.
And the Germans helped them, and they rounded up a mass of people, of Jewish men on that day in 1941.
There are still statues.
And everything was on strike, the whole system spontaneously.
The Dutch train and every transportation-- everything that was in public was stopped after that as protest for the non-Jewish population to our-- their sympathy.
There are statues, and in Amsterdam to that affect.
It was in February 1941.
It was a massive round-up, and it was a horrible situation.
I didn't even attend, I think, the funeral of my grandmother.
I wasn't there.
I don't know what happened.
But she was buried in Holland in the place there, in the cemetery.
But I wasn't, didn't attend.
Yes, she was ill.
She was in a hospital.
And I'm not exactly sure what the illness was.
My mother didn't know.
My mother was very upset.
And naturally like everybody else, not on top of that there were round-ups almost every night around in our neighborhood.
That means the Germans were coming to the houses, designated before who was to be rounded up.
So that was in our neighborhood.
And the people yelled, and screamed, and cried.
And my mother had to sit in the window, in front of the window, and watch during the night.
No matter what we asked her to come or go to bed, she didn't listen.
Then in-- she had her sister living in Amsterdam.
By the way, her sister and her husband were moving back in Amsterdam in 1938.
My mother didn't have too much connection with her sister.
There was something between sisters.
I don't know.
They had an argument.
Then her sister with husband, who was non-Jewish, moved back to Amsterdam, but he developed stomach cancer.
That I remember.
He was very, very ill.
We visited him.
And one-- they lived in a dingy flat at that time.
One thing I remember, he must have been a hunter.
When I entered that room there, there was in the niche here, there was a big bear.
They must have shot.
And I'm telling you, when the first time when I saw that bear, I didn't know.
That was vividly I remember.
Then I came into the bedroom, and I saw he was terribly ill.
He died.
And my sister went-- my aunt went for a short while with us.
But then she got a job as a housekeeper, somewhere in Amsterdam.
I probably keeping in contact, although we didn't have a phone.
No phones were at that time, very rare.
There were phones, but we didn't have one.
Then in 19-- August 1942, there was somebody, who and how notified my mother that her sister was being deported and being held in the Gestapo headquarters in Amsterdam.
My mother was beside herself.

And she run to the corner of our street where we had our physician to try to get a certificate that my aunt was too ill to be deported, or that she had-- That was frequently done with some success, with not success.
That then they get that certificate, the Germans, they release the people.
In our case, it didn't help.
And my mother was actually never conscious when she came back.
The doctor's office returned my mother on a stretcher to our home.
She was put to bed, and we called the doctor again.
And the doctor told us, sorry Mr.
Mossel, we cannot admit your wife because she's Jewish.
And we don't want the Jewish there in the hospital.
What I'd guess and see now, she had a serious stroke.
Oh yes.
She was in a coma, and still was on the bed thrashing around.
And then come quiet, all the bodily functions just went without her knowing.
She was in a coma from that Friday morning till the Monday night at August 31, 1942 when she died.
And in the meantime, I didn't-- when my grandmother died, my grandfather came to live with us.
So here we were, the three of us left alone.
Devastated, I didn't go to the funeral.
I just accompanied the coffin to the end of the street.
Across the street there lived a fascist family.
They never had any decent meals during the whole time when the time was normal.
He was a shoemaker and couldn't make any-- how do you say it in Dutch?
Earn any dry bread, eat never, very poor.
I in the beginning-- sometimes I played with her.
Not during the war, because they showed very definitely that they didn't like us.
They lived right across from us.
And they were laughing when I passed with the coffin of my mother and the whole funeral procession.
They laughed about that.
My father, yes at that time, was not much that in the diamond industry anyway.
And like everybody else, I assume, and I know he did, the people had to make a living.
There was no-- I don't think there was no unemployment insurance.
There was nothing at that time there.
So they made their living in the black market, and a lot of people did that.
So we existed, the three of us afterwards.
We had a very, very friendly neighbor.
The woman married.
He was a Jewish man.
She was not Jewish, but she helped us.
She cooked our meals.
From the day that my mother died, she helped us.
Till we were deported to the camp, she cooked our meals for us.
Could you tell me a little bit more about the Jewish high school?
For instance, where was it located?
It was located in not the central-- almost the central of Amsterdam.
And there was a theater across from us, which showed sometimes a very long time [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a very, very naturally anti-Semitic movie, but it was playing.
And every time that I came out of school that face, what the typical characteristics what they used was on the billboard outside.
What was this movie about?
We were not allowed to go in the movies.
I didn't know.
But I mean, it was not much good.
I'm sure of that.
Oh yeah, and I also-- I had my lunch, because it's too far back to walk to home.
I had my lunch with relatives of mine.
But at that time, I didn't know they were relatives.
I found that out later.
But that was family of my relatives of my grandmother.
I found that out just years ago in Amsterdam going to the register that that was family.
I have never been well informed about our family, but they was very-- I enjoyed it there.
And walking there, I had a good time.
They always gave me a good time.
